## Revealed Beliefs: Methodology

**Key insight**: All model parameters are observable *except* $\lambda$

::: {style="height: 0.5em;"}
:::

**Algorithm**:

1. Set observable parameters to firm-specific values ($r_i$, $\ell_i$, etc.)
2. For each candidate $\lambda$: solve model $\to$ predicted investment intensity
3. Find $\hat{\lambda}$ matching observed CapEx/Revenue

::: {.fragment}
$$\frac{I(K^*(\hat{\lambda}))}{V(X^*(\hat{\lambda}), K^*(\hat{\lambda}))} = \frac{\text{CapEx}_i}{\text{Revenue}_i}$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
In the spirit of @samuelson1938note: infer beliefs from *actions*, not *words*
:::

---

## What Does $\lambda$ Mean?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Expected time to regime switch**: $E[T] = 1/\lambda$

| $\lambda$ | Expected years |
|:---------|:--------------|
| 0.05 | 20 years |
| 0.10 | 10 years |
| 0.20 | 5 years |
| 0.50 | 2 years |
| 1.00 | 1 year |
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Probability of switch within 5 years**:

$$P = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot 5}$$

| $\lambda$ | 5-year prob |
|:---------|:-----------|
| 0.05 | 22% |
| 0.10 | 39% |
| 0.20 | 63% |
| 0.50 | 92% |
| 1.00 | 99% |
:::
:::

---

## Credit Risk

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Credit spreads** increase with leverage:

- $\ell = 0.2$: ~50--100 bps
- $\ell = 0.4$: ~100--200 bps
- $\ell = 0.6$+: 400+ bps
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**5-year default probability**:

- $\ell = 0.2$: 2--4%
- $\ell = 0.4$: 5--8%
- $\ell = 0.6$+: 15%+
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**CoreWeave-like** ($\ell = 0.70$): highest credit risk

$\Rightarrow$ Consistent with elevated yields on CoreWeave debt
:::

---

## The Dario Dilemma {.center}

> What is the cost of investing with the wrong $\lambda$?

---

## The Dario Dilemma: Setup

**Firm has**: true belief $\lambda_{\text{true}}$, invests using $\lambda_{\text{invest}}$

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Conservative** ($\lambda_{\text{invest}} < \lambda_{\text{true}}$):

- Invests too late
- Builds too little
- Misses revenue in boom
- *Lost opportunity*
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Aggressive** ($\lambda_{\text{invest}} > \lambda_{\text{true}}$):

- Invests too early
- Builds too much
- Excess maintenance costs
- Default risk
- *Existential threat*
:::
:::

---

## The Dario Dilemma: Asymmetry

**Proposition 6**: Aggressive overinvestment is costlier than conservative underinvestment

::: {.fragment}
**Value loss**: $\Delta V = \text{NPV}(\lambda_{\text{true}}, \lambda_{\text{true}}) - \text{NPV}(\lambda_{\text{true}}, \lambda_{\text{invest}})$

The loss function is *asymmetric*: steeper on the aggressive side
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Implication**: Observed extreme investment can be rationalized **only** if firms genuinely hold high-$\lambda$ beliefs

$\Rightarrow$ Strategic posturing is too costly to explain the data
:::

---

## Equity Value and $\lambda$

**Equity value is convex in $\lambda$**:

- Increasing: higher $\lambda$ $\to$ more valuable growth option
- Convex: marginal effect of $\lambda$ is increasing
- Steepest around $\lambda \in [0.1, 0.5]$ --- the range of current disagreement

::: {.fragment}
**Portfolio implication**: AI infrastructure stocks should exhibit positive convexity to AI capability news

- Good news raises valuations more than bad news lowers them
:::

---
